content.assessment: This proposal targets the mechanism by which network expansion (adding lower grades, approving new tracks) generates local segregation, directly engaging network-planning concerns about capacity growth and geographic access. It is preventive rather than corrective, meaning it constrains future network configuration without altering existing geographic concentration (F-live-school_network_planning-05) or feeder-school dilution already occurring (F-live-school_network_planning-07). Because expansion approvals are the mechanism by which selective capacity nodes proliferate or intensify locally, a formalized gatekeeping test is plausible as a capacity-growth control, though this lens's evidence base does not directly address the church/private maintainer dynamics central to the proposal's stated rationale.
opportunities: Could halt further deepening of the urban-concentration pattern (F-live-school_network_planning-05) by preventing new expansion in already over-concentrated areas
strengths: Targets the expansion-approval point, which is the network's primary lever for adding new capacity nodes, making it a structurally apt point of intervention for controlling future geographic concentration
threats: Since capacity has already stagnated near its ceiling (F-live-school_network_planning-03), a purely preventive tool may have limited marginal effect if little new expansion is being proposed system-wide, concentrating its real-world impact on a narrow set of edge cases
weaknesses: Does not address already-existing geographic concentration of selective capacity in urban/Budapest schools (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
Does not relieve current feeder-school dilution pressures from already-established selective schools (F-live-school_network_planning-07)
